intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, approachable tone with casual language and encouraging feedback will feel more motivating for personal health tracking than clinical precision
facade.label: Warm welcome
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers conversational, supportive voice—use exclamation points, casual phrasing, celebration of small wins
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers neutral, matter-of-fact tone—minimize personality, stick to facts and data, clinical language acceptable

evidence.hypothesis: A friendly, approachable tone with casual language and encouraging feedback will feel more motivating for personal health tracking than clinical precision
evidence.implication: User prefers conversational, supportive voice—use exclamation points, casual phrasing, celebration of small wins

facade.hypothesis: A stripped-down, numbers-first interface with high information density and reduced visual ornamentation will feel clean and cerebral rather than warm or approachable.
facade.label: Minimalist data view
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward clinical precision, prefers function over feeling, comfortable with stark layouts. May also signal preference for cool tone overall.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants some warmth, visual breathing room, or human touch in how data is presented. Suggests desire for more organic, less purely digital aesthetic.

facade.hypothesis: For long-term health experiments, users prefer glanceable weekly roll-ups (key metrics + trend arrow) over detailed daily data tables
facade.axisTargeted: Aggregation level: granular daily logs vs. rolled-up summaries
facade.label: Weekly summary cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values quick pattern recognition and decision-making over exhaustive data review; dashboard should prioritize weekly/monthly views with drill-down options
facade.rejectImplies: User needs to see every data point and daily variance; dashboard should default to chronological tables or day-by-day timelines with aggregation as secondary

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard feels more approachable and less clinical with curved UI elements rather than sharp angles
facade.axisTargeted: Warmth vs. clinical coldness in visual treatment
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers a softer, more human-friendly aesthetic for health tracking—avoid hard edges and sharp transitions
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clarity and precision over softness—may favor clean lines, grids, and defined boundaries that feel more analytical

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar for experiment navigation paired with card-based layouts for individual experiment data will feel organized without overwhelming the dashboard.
facade.axisTargeted: Layout structure: sidebar vs tabs, and density of information
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values clear navigation hierarchy and prefers to see multiple experiments simultaneously in a scannable card format.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer full-width tabs or list-based views where experiments feel more sequential or interconnected rather than compartmentalized.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Animated content transitions (rather than instant refreshes) make health data feel less jarring and more understandable
facade.label: Smooth slide-in updates
facade.acceptImplies: User values gentle, continuous feedback over fast-loading discrete states—likely wants to *watch* their data unfold
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers instant clarity and speed over visual continuity—probably wants to scan and act quickly without animation overhead

facade.hypothesis: A voice that actively celebrates milestones and small wins with enthusiastic language will feel more motivating than neutral, matter-of-fact feedback during health tracking
facade.label: Celebratory tone
facade.acceptImplies: Use congratulatory language, emojis, and upbeat phrasing when users log activities or hit targets; lean into dopamine-hit moments
facade.rejectImplies: Maintain steady, non-emotional tone focused on data accuracy and clinical relevance; avoid celebratory language as potentially frivolous or disingenuous

draft.title: Experiment Tracker Dashboard
draft.summary: A personal health dashboard for monitoring long-term experiments with visual progress tracking, experiment cards, and key metrics at a glance.
draft.html:

Day 17 of 40

Intermittent Fasting

16:8 fasting window, tracking energy levels

Day 11 of 40
